questions.question: Which `position` value removes an element from the normal document flow AND positions it relative to the browser viewport (so it stays in place when you scroll)?
options: position: absolute
position: relative
position: fixed
position: sticky
questions.explanation: `position: fixed` removes the element from the normal flow and positions it relative to the viewport. It stays in place even when the page scrolls — that's why it's used for fixed navigation bars and floating buttons.

questions.question: What does `z-index` control, and what is required for it to work?
options: It controls element width, and works on any element
It controls stacking order (which element appears on top), and only works on positioned elements (not `static`)
It controls the zoom level of an element, and works on all elements
It controls stacking order, and works on all elements including `position: static`
questions.explanation: `z-index` controls which overlapping element appears on top (the stacking order). Higher values sit on top of lower values. It only works on elements with a `position` value other than `static` — meaning `relative`, `absolute`, `fixed`, or `sticky`.

questions.question: In a Flexbox container with the default `flex-direction: row`, what does `justify-content` control?
options: The vertical alignment of items
The horizontal distribution/spacing of items along the main axis
The order in which items appear
Whether items wrap to the next line
questions.explanation: `justify-content` controls how items are distributed along the main axis. With the default `flex-direction: row`, the main axis is horizontal, so `justify-content` controls horizontal spacing — values like `center`, `space-between`, and `flex-end` move items left and right.

questions.question: What is the difference between `justify-content` and `align-items` in Flexbox?
options: `justify-content` works on the main axis, `align-items` works on the cross axis (perpendicular to the main axis)
They do the same thing but for different browsers
`justify-content` only works with `flex-direction: row`, `align-items` only works with `flex-direction: column`
`justify-content` works on individual items, `align-items` works on the container
questions.explanation: `justify-content` aligns items along the main axis (horizontal by default), while `align-items` aligns items along the cross axis (vertical by default). When you change `flex-direction` to `column`, the axes swap — `justify-content` becomes vertical and `align-items` becomes horizontal.

questions.question: Which Flexbox property do you set on the container to allow items to wrap to the next line when they don't fit?
options: flex-flow: wrap
flex-wrap: wrap
flex-direction: wrap
overflow: wrap
questions.explanation: `flex-wrap: wrap` allows flex items to wrap to the next line instead of shrinking to fit on one line. The default is `nowrap`, which forces all items to stay on a single line.

questions.question: What does `grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr)` create?
options: A single column that is 3 fractions wide
Three columns, each taking one-third of the available space
Three rows of equal height
A grid that repeats three times
questions.explanation: `repeat(3, 1fr)` creates three columns, each getting one fraction (1fr) of the available space — so each column is one-third of the container width. The `fr` unit distributes space proportionally, and `repeat()` is shorthand to avoid writing `1fr 1fr 1fr`.

questions.question: What does the `fr` unit represent in CSS Grid?
options: A fixed unit equal to 16 pixels
A fraction of the remaining available space in the grid container
The full row height
A percentage of the viewport width
questions.explanation: The `fr` unit stands for 'fraction' and represents a share of the available space in the grid container. `1fr 2fr` means the second column gets twice as much space as the first. The browser calculates `fr` values after subtracting any fixed-size tracks (like `200px`).

questions.question: What does `grid-template-areas` allow you to do?
options: Set the background color for different grid regions
Name regions of your grid layout and assign elements to them using `grid-area`
Automatically generate grid columns based on content
Add padding inside grid cells
questions.explanation: `grid-template-areas` lets you define named regions in your grid layout using a visual text map. You then assign elements to those regions with `grid-area: region-name`. It makes your layout code readable — you can literally see the page structure in your CSS.

questions.question: When should you choose CSS Grid over Flexbox?
options: When centering a single element
When you need a two-dimensional layout with control over both rows and columns simultaneously
When you have only two or three items to align
When you need items to wrap to the next line
questions.explanation: CSS Grid excels at two-dimensional layouts — when you need to control both rows and columns at the same time. Page layouts with headers, sidebars, content areas, and footers are a natural fit. Flexbox is better for one-dimensional layouts (a single row or column of items).

questions.question: What is the difference between `auto-fit` and `auto-fill` in `grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(250px, 1fr))`?
options: There is no difference — they are aliases for the same behavior
`auto-fit` is for rows and `auto-fill` is for columns
`auto-fit` collapses empty tracks so items stretch to fill the space, while `auto-fill` keeps empty tracks even when there aren't enough items to fill them
`auto-fill` only works with the `fr` unit, `auto-fit` works with any unit
questions.explanation: Both create as many columns as will fit, but they differ when there aren't enough items to fill all columns. `auto-fit` collapses the empty tracks, letting existing items expand to fill the container. `auto-fill` keeps the empty tracks, so items don't stretch beyond their maximum size. In most cases, `auto-fit` is what you want.
